frictionScore.recommendations: Answer common student questions without a human — currently 0/12 typical questions are resolved by the IVR; every one routes to a department, putting callers on hold for answers an automated system could provide instantly; Cut average call resolution time from 2 min 52 sec to under 10 sec — Keel routes by intent in a single conversational turn instead of a 2-level digit menu; Make operators 24/7 — every operator-staffed department is currently gated to Monday–Friday business hours, stranding evenings, weekends, and after-hours callers; Eliminate the 3:05 submenu prompt — replace 'press 1 for admissions, press 2 for financial aid' with natural-language intent capture
frictionScore.executive_summary:
**Executive Summary — California State University, Sacramento IVR Assessment**

The current IVR scores **78/100 (Poor)** on caller-experience friction. The defining problem isn't the menu structure — the IVR is shallow (2 levels). It's that **0 of 12 typical student questions** can be answered without a human. Every caller asking about hours, application status, tuition balance, transcripts, or course registration gets routed to a department and waits on hold for an answer the IVR could have given them instantly.

That hold time is real: callers wait an average of **2 min 52 sec** to reach a human, the submenu prompt alone runs **3:05** before any digit press is accepted, and every operator-staffed department is gated to **Monday–Friday business hours**. Evening, weekend, and non-English callers are effectively locked out.

Keel's voice agent collapses this into a single conversational turn. The agent itself answers ~10 of the 12 typical questions directly — hours, status, billing, FAQs — and only escalates to a human when judgment is needed (academic standing, advisor specifics). Operates 24/7, supports 25+ languages, and never makes a caller listen to a 3-minute menu prompt. Projected friction lands at **10/100 (Excellent)** — a **~68-point reduction** driven primarily by closing the question-coverage gap, cutting resolution time by ~95%, and removing the after-hours dependency.

frictionScore.recommendations: Answer common student questions without a human — currently 0/12 typical questions are resolved by the IVR; every one routes to a department, dead-ends, or repeats the menu; Add a 'press 0 for operator' option at every menu level — currently no operator-zero exists, so callers who can't navigate the digit menu have no way to reach a human; Eliminate the 3 dead-end paths (digits 1, 3, 5) — these spin callers through repeated prompts and end the call without a resolution; Cut average call resolution time from 2 min 9 sec to under 10 sec — Keel routes by intent in a single conversational turn
frictionScore.executive_summary:
**Executive Summary — San José State University IVR Assessment**

The current IVR scores **68/100 (Poor)** on caller-experience friction. Three problems stack: (1) **0 of 12** typical student questions can be answered without a human — every option routes to a department; (2) **no operator-zero** exists, so callers who don't recognize their need in the 6-option main menu have no escape hatch; (3) **3 of 6 tested paths dead-end** — callers hear repeated prompts and the call ends without a resolution.

Average call duration is **2 min 09 sec**, and operator-staffed paths are gated to **Monday–Friday business hours**. Evening, weekend, and non-English callers are effectively locked out.

Keel's voice agent collapses this into a single conversational turn. The agent answers ~10 of the 12 typical questions directly, escalates to a human when judgment is needed, operates 24/7, and supports 25+ languages. Projected friction lands at **10/100 (Excellent)**.

frictionScore.recommendations: Add a digit menu so callers can route themselves to the right department instead of waiting on hold for a student to manually transfer them; Add self-service for common questions — 0 of 12 typical student questions can be answered without a human, even hours/locations/payment status; Add an operator-zero option AND a 24/7 escalation path — student workers are limited to business hours, and there is currently NO way to reach the IVR after-hours; Reduce reliance on student workers — a single forced queue means every caller waits for the same handful of students, and complex queries get bounced to a second queue (doubling the wait)
frictionScore.executive_summary:
**Executive Summary — Santa Clara University IVR Assessment**

The current IVR scores **68/100 (Poor)** on caller-experience friction. The defining problem is structural: Santa Clara has **no IVR menu at all**. Every inbound caller — regardless of why they're calling — is dropped into a **single forced hold queue** answered by 'the next available student worker'. There are no options to press, no self-service paths, no operator-zero shortcut, no after-hours fallback, and no parallel routing. A caller asking about office hours waits in the same queue as a caller with a complex billing dispute.

Three concrete failure modes:
1. **Zero caller agency** — without a menu, callers cannot signal their intent before talking to a human. The student worker has to triage every call manually, often transferring to a second queue and doubling the wait.
2. **No self-service** — 0 of 12 typical student questions (hours, application status, tuition balance, transcript requests, account help, etc.) can be answered without a human. Callers wait on hold for answers an automated system could deliver in seconds.
3. **Single point of failure** — when student workers are busy, every caller waits in the same queue. There is no after-hours coverage, no multilingual support, and no overflow path.

Keel's voice agent eliminates all three. The agent answers ~10 of the 12 typical questions directly, escalates to a human only when judgment is needed, operates 24/7, and supports 25+ languages. Projected friction lands at **10/100 (Excellent)** — a **~63-point reduction** that converts every inbound call from a hold-queue gamble into a sub-10-second resolution.
